title: Filipino Sinigang (Tamarind Prawn Soup)

subs.name: 400 g pork ribs (pre-boiled 10 minutes) for a non-seafood sinigang
subs.notes: increase simmering to 25 minutes after the tamarind is added

ingredients.name: tamarind pulp
visual_checks: dissolve in 200 ml warm water and strain; adjust for sourness
ingredients.notes: dissolve in 200 ml warm water and strain; adjust for sourness

subs.name: 3 tbsp lemon juice or 2 tbsp amchur dissolved in water
subs.notes: gives the sour note though without the earthy tamarind complexity

ingredients.name: radish or carrot, cut in 1 cm rounds
visual_checks: white radish (daikon/mooli) is traditional; carrot is widely available
ingredients.notes: white radish (daikon/mooli) is traditional; carrot is widely available

subs.name: chopped drumstick leaves (murungai keerai), amaranth or any green
subs.notes: drumstick leaves give a South Indian flavour bridge

ingredients.name: green chilli, whole
visual_checks: add whole for mild heat; slit for more spice
ingredients.notes: add whole for mild heat; slit for more spice

subs.name: 2 tbsp soy sauce plus a pinch of salt

steps.text: Dissolve the tamarind pulp in 200 ml warm water. Squeeze and knead to extract the juice, then strain through a sieve, discarding the fibres. Set the sour water aside.
Heat oil in a large pot on medium heat. Add the onion and tomato and sauté for 3–4 minutes until the tomato softens and releases its juice into the pot.
Pour in 1 litre of water and the strained tamarind liquid. Add fish sauce and bring to a steady boil.
Add the carrot (or radish) and green beans to the boiling broth. Simmer for 6–8 minutes until just tender but not mushy.
Add the prawn and green chillies. Simmer for 3–4 minutes until the prawn turn pink and curl. Do not overcook — they should be just done.

Drop in the spinach leaves, stir once and remove from heat after 30 seconds — the residual heat wilts the spinach perfectly. Taste the broth: adjust sourness with more tamarind water, saltiness with fish sauce, and heat with chilli.

Ladle generously into deep bowls and serve with steamed rice on the side. In Filipino tradition, the rice is dipped into the sinigang broth itself — the soup is as much a sauce for the rice as a standalone dish.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Cook seafood until safely done for the species and preparation; keep it chilled before cooking.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: prawn → 400 g pork ribs (pre-boiled 10 minutes) for a non-seafood sinigang (increase simmering to 25 minutes after the tamarind is added); tamarind → 3 tbsp lemon juice or 2 tbsp amchur dissolved in water (gives the sour note though without the earthy tamarind complexity); fish-sauce → 2 tbsp soy sauce plus a pinch of salt; spinach → chopped drumstick leaves (murungai keerai), amaranth or any green (drumstick leaves give a South Indian flavour bridge)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Sinigang maps very naturally to a South Indian palate — the sour tamarind broth is familiar territory. Use tamarind pulp (imli/puli) from any Indian pantry. Daikon radish (mooli) is widely available in North India; carrot substitutes perfectly in South India. Drumstick (murungakkai) is an excellent South Indian addition — add with the beans. Fish sauce (available at Big Basket or specialty stores) adds depth; soy sauce is a workable alternative.
